local_names.romanized_local: Ketumbar Halus (Malay); Pisa hua dhaniya (Hindi romanized)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of ground coriander seeds is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2.5 g), used as a spice in cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Ground coriander seeds — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for ground coriander seeds (CR ID F10060030). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for coriander seed (FDC ID 171319) for secondary validation of saturated fat and micronutrient values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ground coriander seeds in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 33 (low) based on the analogous food category of dried leguminous seeds and spices with very high dietary fibre (41.9 g/100g) and low net available carbohydrate (8.4 g/100g). Glycemic load per 2.5 g serving = (33 × 8.4 / 100) × 0.025 ≈ 0.07, rounded to 0.1. This is clinically negligible. Estimation basis: Brand-Miller et al. (2008), Diabetes Care, and University of Sydney GI Database methodology for high-fibre spice analogues.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (35 mg/100g) with exceptionally high potassium (1267 mg/100g), yielding a strongly favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Ground coriander seeds are highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; used in typical culinary quantities of 1–2 teaspoons per dish, absolute sodium contribution is negligible (<1 mg per serving) while potassium and dietary fibre support blood pressure regulation — avoid pre-mixed spice blends that may contain added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high dietary fibre (41.9 g/100g), low net available carbohydrate (8.4 g/100g), and bioactive compounds (linalool, quercetin, flavonoids) with evidence-based hypoglycaemic and insulin-sensitising properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ground coriander seeds are appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes; the estimated GI is low (~33) and per-serving carbohydrate contribution is negligible at typical culinary doses, while phytochemical constituents may support improved insulin secretion and glucose uptake — avoid coriander-based spice blends with added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1 g/100g), zero cholesterol, very high dietary fibre (41.9 g/100g), and high potassium (1267 mg/100g) with anti-inflammatory polyphenols (quercetin, linalool) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ground coriander seeds are cardioprotective when used as a culinary spice; their negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high fibre, and anti-inflammatory phytochemical profile support lipid management and vascular health — use as a flavour base in place of high-sodium seasonings.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols (linalool, quercetin) reduce inflammatory cytokines; high calcium (709 mg/100g) and potassium (1267 mg/100g) support bone and joint health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ground coriander seeds may provide adjunctive anti-inflammatory benefit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when incorporated consistently into a spice-rich diet; avoid pre-mixed blends with added salt or anti-caking agents.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ground coriander seeds (Coriandrum sativum) are a minimally processed spice produced by drying and finely grinding the ripe seeds of the coriander plant. The resulting powder is a fine, tan-coloured, dry product with a warm, citrusy, slightly floral and earthy aromatic profile. It is one of the most widely used spices globally, featuring prominently in South Asian, Middle Eastern, Southeast Asian, North African, and Latin American cuisines. In Singapore and Malaysia, it is a foundational spice in curry pastes, rendang, satay marinades, and spice rubs. Nutritionally, ground coriander seeds are exceptionally rich in dietary fibre (41.9 g/100g), making them one of the highest-fibre spices available. They provide meaningful protein (12.4 g/100g), moderate fat (17.8 g/100g, predominantly monounsaturated), and a low net available carbohydrate content (8.4 g/100g). Micronutrient density is high, with notable calcium (709 mg/100g), potassium (1267 mg/100g), and iron (16.32 mg/100g). Bioactive compounds including linalool, quercetin, and other flavonoids confer evidence-based anti-inflammatory, hypoglycaemic, and antihypertensive properties. The glycemic index is estimated as low (~33) and glycemic load per typical serving is clinically negligible. Ground coriander seeds are suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Typical culinary serving is 1–2 teaspoons (2.5–5 g) per dish.
common_user_queries: Is ground coriander good for diabetes?
Can I use coriander seeds if I have high blood pressure?
What are the health benefits of ground coriander?
How much fibre is in coriander powder?
Is coriander powder vegan and gluten-free?
What is the difference between coriander seeds and coriander powder?
meal_context: spice rub for meats and fish
Middle Eastern spice mixes (baharat, ras el hanout)

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F10060030, confidence 0.95), an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat and additional micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171319) for coriander seed. GI is an analogous estimate documented with methodology. Ontology codes are defensible and cross-checked.
notes: selenium_mcg set to -1: selenium data for ground coriander seeds is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for coriander seed (FDC 171319) with sufficient specificity for this preparation. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero for dried ground spice per USDA FoodData Central). vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, and vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 (confirmed absent or negligible in plant-derived dried spice per USDA FoodData Central FDC 171319). vitamin_a_mcg_rae reported as 0.0 per USDA FoodData Central for coriander seed (RAE negligible in seed form vs. coriander leaf). Saturated fat value of 1.0 g/100g is sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC 171319 (coriander seed) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; this is documented as an adapted value. Monounsaturated fat (13.6 g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (1.8 g/100g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC 171319. GI of ~33 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for ground coriander seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input has been removed as ground coriander seeds are a spice, not a grain product. The input condition key 'diabetes' has been mapped to the required key 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas rules.
